concepts.name: Technology Mudslide Hypothesis
concepts.definition: The hypothesis that relentless technological change makes it impossible for leading firms to stay on top. Christensen disproved this: neither pace nor difficulty of change caused failures.

concepts.name: Sustaining Technologies
concepts.definition: Technologies that sustain established performance trajectories. They make good products better in ways existing customers value. Incumbents nearly always lead.

concepts.name: Disruptive Technologies
concepts.definition: Technologies that are typically simpler, cheaper, and initially underperform on dimensions mainstream customers value. They offer a different value proposition for emerging or overlooked markets.

concepts.name: The Innovator's Dilemma
concepts.definition: The paradox that the same management practices that make companies industry leaders — listening to best customers, investing in highest-margin opportunities — also cause them to miss disruptive technologies. Good management can be the root cause of failure.

concepts.name: RPV Theory (Resources, Processes, Values)
concepts.definition: An organization's capabilities and disabilities are defined by its resources (what it has), processes (how it works), and values (what it prioritizes). These define what an organization can and cannot do.

arguments.claim: The technology mudslide hypothesis is wrong. Leading firms failed not because of pace or difficulty of technological change.
arguments.evidence: In every case of sustaining technology change (radical or incremental), established firms led. But in disruptive technology changes, entrants almost always won. The pattern is consistent across the HDD industry's 5 architectural generations.

arguments.claim: Good management practices — not bad management — caused leading firms to fail at disruptive technologies
arguments.evidence: Firms listened to their best customers (who didn't want the simpler technology), invested in highest-margin opportunities (disruption starts in low-margin markets), and pursued larger markets (disruption starts small). Each of these is sound management. The problem is structural, not incompetent.

cases.name: HDD Industry 1965-1995
cases.context: Five architectural generations: 14-inch → 8-inch → 5.25-inch → 3.5-inch → 2.5-inch. Each generation's leaders never won the next.
cases.illustrates: The pattern: incumbents lead in sustaining innovation (making existing drives better for existing customers) but entrants lead in disruptive innovation (introducing smaller, initially lower-capacity drives for new applications).
cases.lesson: The pattern had no exceptions across 5 generations — it was structural, not random.

logicChains.description: Mainstream customers reject disruptive technology (too low performance on dimensions they value) → Managers rationally listen and ignore it → Disruptive technology finds foothold in new market (where its different attributes are valued) → Technology improves on its trajectory → Eventually meets mainstream needs → Incumbent enters too late

terminology.definition: The rate at which a technology improves along a measurable dimension over time.
A market, often emerging or non-consumption-based, where a disruptive technology first takes hold because its different attributes are valued there.

nuance: Disruption is NOT about bad technology losing to good technology. It's about different definitions of performance for different markets.
The failure is NOT a management mistake — it's a rational outcome of the resource allocation process.
